This is a 21-year-old female, who has a problem dating back to 12/04/23, currently with a chief complaint of neck and mid and lower back pain, headaches, dizziness, shoulder pain, and elbow pain; referred by self.

- Concerning the onset of symptoms, the patient reports that on 12/04/23, she was the driver of a motor vehicle. She was traveling in traffic on the freeway when she was subsequently rear-ended by a semi-truck. She states that this caused her car to spin out of control, and then she hit the barrier. She notes that she hit her head on the steering wheel and feels like she lost consciousness for a brief period of time. She was wearing a seatbelt. She is unsure if the airbags deployed. The car was totaled.

- She subsequently presented to Harborview Medical Center where she had a CT scan of her head and x-rays taken that per her report did not demonstrate any evidence of fracture or acute injury. She was told at Harborview that she also had a concussion.

- At this time, the patient reports that her primary concern today is her headaches and dizziness. She notes that the headaches feel like they involve her whole head. She also has nausea and vomiting. She notes that she has significant trouble sleeping at night, only getting 1-2 hours of sleep. She is also napping during the day. The patient also has difficulty with her concentration. She has been unable to return to work.

Description Of Procedure: Bilateral Greater Occipital nerve block
Verbal informed consent was obtained and the risks, benefits, and alternatives were discussed with the patient who agrees with the following procedure.
The patient was placed in a prone position and prepped in a normal fashion using Betadine.
The midpoint between the union and the mastoid process on the right was identified by palpation. Using ultrasound guidance the greater occipital nerve was identified at this site. Using 1cc (40 mg) of kenalog and 1 cc of 1% lidocaine for the injection medication, a 27-gauge 1.5-inch needle was inserted at this site until os was reached. Aspiration revealed negative heme. Injection of 1.0cc total (half of the aforementioned doses) was done, the medication was injected smoothly. The needle was removed and the site of injection cleaned and dried. This was then repeated on the contralateral side.
Procedure Codes: 64405 N BLOCK INJ, OCCIPITAL, Units: 2.00 , 76942 ULTRASOUND GUIDANCE FOR NEEDLE PLACEMENT, J3301 INJ TRIAMCINOLONE ACETONIDE 10 MG
Follow Up: 3 Weeks (Reason: f/u w/ Greeshma (can add on TPI))

PLAN:
- In terms of the patient's headaches, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and difficulty with sleep, she likely sustained a concussion and continues to have postconcussive symptoms. To help managing her difficulty sleeping and her pain at night, we prescribed cyclobenzaprine. The patient is also counseled to try melatonin and to take this a couple hours before she intends to go to sleep. She was also counseled to limit her daytime naps to try to reset her normal sleep/wake patterns. Would likely move toward brain injury testing as the next step, but given the patient's significant symptoms at this time, she would likely not be able to tolerate the exam very well.
- In terms of the patient's neck and upper/mid back pain, this is multifactorial with a combination of myofascial pain and facet/ligamentous injury. Radicular symptoms are also possible, but she does not demonstrate significant neurologic deficits to warrant more urgent imaging.
- We first decided to start with trigger point injections to help with the myofascial component of her pain, particularly as she has been unable to tolerate massage very well. We will follow up with the patient at the next available appointment for this.
- In terms of the patient's lower back pain, this is multifactorial with a combination of myofascial pain, facet/ligamentous injury, and SI joint-mediated pain. Radicular symptoms are also on the differential. Otherwise, she does not demonstrate significant neurologic deficits to warrant more urgent imaging. Recommend to continue with conservative treatment as above.
- In terms of the patient's left-greater-than-right anterolateral shoulder pain, suspect a component of rotator cuff injury/subacromial bursitis. Would like to see how her shoulder pain improves as the myofascial component of her pain improves, but if symptoms persist, would consider an MRI, particularly of the left shoulder, and potential injection intervention thereafter.
- In terms of the patient's left-greater-than-right elbow pain, suspect a component of lateral epicondylitis. We will continue to monitor for now.
- The patient should otherwise continue with chiropractic care and start massage therapy once able to tolerate this.

• This RightEye Dynamic Vision Report is designed to provide an assessment of your vision as reflected by oculomotor behavior.

• Processing Speed (ms): the average difference between when the eye hits the target (e.g.: alien) and the button is pressed. Lower is better.
• Reaction Time (ms): the average time difference between when the arrow begins shooting from the solar system and the button is pressed. Lower is better. Appendix

Low Yield (█): This is reported when the amount and/or quality of the acquired data are insufficient to generate an accurate number. This may result from the presence of one or more artifact sources such as motion, sweating, poor electrode-scalp contact, or interference from nearby electronic equipment.
Questionable Value ( ? ): Possibly due to low Alpha or Peak Frequency magnitude relative to background EEG noise.

Excess Synchrony (┆): A type of artifact which may affect multiple channels equally at the same time. This may be due to improper electrode connections or environmental interference. Excess Synchrony can reduce the accuracy of background EEG metrics.
Manually Modified Artifacting (★): Shown next to sessions in which at least one input file has manually modified artifacting.

Sync Blinks: Short for "synchronized eye blinks," this is reported when FP1 or FP2 is greater than or equal to 20 V. Sync Blinks may affect the reported P300 and/or Flanker test depths and latencies at other electrode locations.
P300 Metrics
Physical Reaction Time: The average time of the physical response to rare tones, derived from mouse or keyboard input.
• Reported as "N/A" if there were less than 15 physical responses to rare tones.
Audio P300 Delay and Audio P300 Voltage metrics are derived from Central-Parietal (C-P) locations CZ, C3, C4, PZ, P3, and P4 with sufficient yield.
• For these metrics, "yield" is defined as the number of brain responses to rare tones which contain minimal artifact.
Audio P300 Delay: The fastest C-P latency between 240-499 ms after a rare tone, among locations that are at least 3 V.
• Reported as "N/A" if no C-P location is at least 3 V, or no C-P location has a yield of at least 20 rare events.
Audio P300 Voltage: The largest C-P amplitude between 240-499 ms after a rare tone.

• Reported as "N/A" if no C-P location has a yield of at least 20 rare events.
• Reported as "< 0 V" if the voltage at all C-P locations is less than 0 V.
Low Yield (█) is shown next to values for Audio P300 Delay or Audio P300 Voltage if:

• Less than 3 C-P locations have a yield of at least 30; OR
• 40% or more data segments contain excessive Delta artifact at the location from which the metric was derived.
Background EEG Metrics
Metrics include CZ Theta/Beta, F3/F4 Alpha, Coherence, Muscle Tension, Peak Frequency. For eyes closed metrics, P300 needs to be run, otherwise reported as "N/A".
Low Yield (█) is shown next to a background EEG metric if:

• Less than 30 segments of data are clean or contain minimal artifact; OR
• For metrics using Delta or Theta, over 40% of data segments contain excessive Delta artifact.
"N/A" is reported for a background EEG metric if:

• Less than 20 segments of data are clean or contain minimal artifact; OR
• For metrics using Delta or Theta, over 50% of data segments contain excessive Delta artifact.
